one_line_takeaway: A contrastively-trained, whole-text semantic mapping model lets a post-hoc LLM watermark be deliberately fragile to malicious sentiment/toxic edits (defeating piggyback spoofing) while staying robust to benign paraphrase — the first watermark to clearly win on security without collapsing detectability, at the cost of somewhat lower paraphrase robustness.
key_finding: The method is the only one of the five that actually removes the watermark under spoofing while keeping it under paraphrase. On Llama-3.1-8B / C4 it drives sentiment-spoof ROC-AUC to 34.68 and hate-speech-spoof to 34.38 (lower is better; the watermark is stripped by the malicious edit), versus KGW at 98.85 / 100.00 where the watermark fully survives the attack. This yields the best Overall AUC (75.23 vs 43.46 for KGW and 45.88 for UNIGRAM) despite a deliberately lower paraphrase-robustness AUC (71.97 vs POSTMARK's 89.03, the best among baselines). The whole-text (GLOBAL) conditioning is essential: the PREFIX variant fails when attacks are confined to the latter half of the text (spoof AUC ~0.97-1.00) whereas GLOBAL stays ~0.34-0.37. Contrastive training is also essential: a general pre-trained sentiment model gives spoof AUC 96.65-100.00 (vulnerable) vs 34.38-34.68 after contrastive training. It is additionally more resistant to stealing (top-k decryption rate <=0.66 vs KGW's 0.80-0.82).
core_contribution: The paper proposes a semantic-aware, post-hoc LLM text-watermarking algorithm designed specifically to resist spoofing attacks (chiefly the piggyback spoofing attack of Pang et al. 2024, where an attacker maliciously alters watermarked text into hate speech or sentiment-reversed content while the watermark survives, letting them falsely attribute harmful text to the LLM provider). Its two-part own contribution over prior semantic watermarking (esp. ADAPTIVE / Liu & Bu 2024, which it explicitly builds on) is: (1) a learned semantic mapping model f(x) that produces the green-red token split from the ENTIRE target text x rather than only the preceding tokens y<t, so a semantic distortion anywhere in the text changes the split and removes the watermark; and (2) training that mapping model with a contrastive triplet loss so its embedding is insensitive to permissible (meaning-preserving) edits like paraphrase/synonym swaps but sensitive to impermissible (meaning-distorting) edits like sentiment reversal and toxic insertion. Watermarking is embedded by paraphrasing the target text with the watermarked LLM (post-hoc), scaling up green-token logits when output entropy exceeds a threshold.
threat_model: Adversary who takes a legitimately watermarked text and wants the watermark to remain detectable AFTER a malicious semantic change, in order to frame the LLM provider as the author of harmful content. Concretely: (a) piggyback spoofing via sentiment reversal (full-text or restricted to the latter half) and via hate-speech/toxic phrase insertion (verified unsafe by Llama Guard 3); and (b) watermark-stealing attacks (Jovanovic et al. 2024) that query the algorithm many times and use token-frequency statistics to infer the green-red split and forge watermarked text. The defender simultaneously wants to keep the watermark ROBUST to benign removal attacks (paraphrasing/synonym substitution) and preserve text quality — so security must be gained without sacrificing detectability, robustness, or quality. A defense is successful when a spoofed/forged text is NO LONGER detected as watermarked (low ROC-AUC on spoofed text) while genuine and paraphrased watermarked text still is (high ROC-AUC).
limitations: (1) A genuine robustness trade-off: paraphrase-removal AUC (~68-81) is markedly lower than POSTMARK's best-among-baselines 89.03, i.e. the same sensitivity that defeats spoofing makes benign paraphrase more likely to (falsely) look like removal. (2) The no-target-text extension is notably weaker — on LFQA the sentiment spoof only drops to 57.57 (hate-speech spoof 50.22) and Overall AUC is 65.40 (Table 8), so the strong defense is clearest in the pure post-hoc setting. (3) Heavy reliance on GPT-4o (and Llama Guard 3) to synthesize training triplets, so the learned notion of 'impermissible' is bounded by those generators and by sentiment/hate categories; other semantic distortions (e.g., factual negation without sentiment change) are untested. (4) Detection reconstructs the split from the candidate text itself, an approximation the paper acknowledges. (5) Evaluation is modest in scale (200 texts per dataset, two 7-8B backbones, two datasets) and the mapping-model encoder is a small sentiment model. (6) Added inference cost from running the whole text through the mapping model at each step is not quantified.

datasets_used: realnewslike subset of C4 (Raffel et al. 2020) - 200 evaluation target texts; original document used as target x
LFQA (Krishna et al. 2023) - 200 evaluation target texts; annotated gold completion used as target x
Mapping-model training set from a separate (non-overlapping) C4 chunk: 8,201 anchor texts, 16 positive + 3 negative texts per anchor, 500-example validation set
quantitative_results: OURS, Llama-3.1-8B / C4: Detectability AUC 98.02, Paraphrased 71.97, Sentiment Spoof 34.68 (down), Hate Speech Spoof 34.38 (down), Overall AUC 75.23, PPL 8.8
OURS, Llama-3.1-8B / LFQA: Detectability 99.16, Paraphrased 80.99, Sentiment Spoof 29.23, Hate Speech Spoof 29.89, Overall AUC 80.26
OURS, Qwen2.5-7B / C4: Detectability 95.80, Paraphrased 67.09, Sentiment Spoof 32.54, Hate Speech Spoof 18.58, Overall AUC 77.94
KGW baseline, Llama / C4: Detectability 100.00, Paraphrased 72.68, Sentiment Spoof 98.85, Hate Speech Spoof 100.00, Overall AUC 43.46 (watermark survives spoofing)

POSTMARK, Llama / C4: best paraphrase robustness among baselines (Paraphrased 89.03) but spoofing survives (Sentiment Spoof 94.07 / Hate Speech Spoof 99.87), Overall AUC 48.77, PPL 9.21, plus worst text quality
LLM-as-judge (1-3 scale), Llama: OURS Text Quality 2.821 / Relevance 2.378, comparable to KGW 2.830 / 2.412; POSTMARK worst at 2.230 / 1.811
Stealing-attack top-k decryption rate (lower=more secure): OURS 0.66 / 0.53 / 0.63 at k=50/100/200 vs KGW 0.80 / 0.82 / 0.82
Ablation GLOBAL vs PREFIX (C4 spoof AUC): GLOBAL ~0.34-0.37 across random & latter-half attacks; PREFIX fails on latter-half (sentiment 0.97, hate 1.00)
Ablation contrastive vs pre-trained mapping model: pre-trained sentiment spoof AUC 96.65 (C4)/93.18 (LFQA) and hate spoof 100.00; contrastive-trained 34.68/29.23 and 34.38/29.89
No-target-text extension (Llama / LFQA, Table 8): OURS Detectability 99.09, Paraphrased 70.30, Sentiment Spoof 57.57, Hate Speech Spoof 50.22, Overall AUC 65.40 (weaker spoof defense)
baselines_compared: KGW (Kirchenbauer et al. 2023) - previous-token hash green-red split, delta=3.0
UNIGRAM (Zhao et al. 2023) - fixed global green-red split, delta=3.0
ADAPTIVE (Liu & Bu 2024) - prefix semantic-representation split with entropy-adaptive watermarking (alpha=2.0, delta0=0.1, delta=0.13); the closest prior work the paper builds on
POSTMARK (Chang et al. 2024) - post-hoc insertion of input-dependent words, ratio=0.06
Internal ablation baselines: PREFIX variant (prefix-only context) and a general pre-trained sentiment mapping model (twitter-roberta-base-sentiment without contrastive fine-tuning)
